lessons.title: Recognizing Online Grooming
lessons.summary: Learn the warning signs of online grooming and how predators build trust with children.

sections.heading: What is Grooming?
sections.body: Grooming is when someone builds a relationship, trust, and emotional connection with a child so they can manipulate, exploit, or abuse them. It happens gradually and can be hard to recognize.

sections.heading: Common Warning Signs
sections.body: Excessive attention or gift-giving. Asking the child to keep secrets. Isolating the child from friends and family. Discussing sexual topics. Asking for private photos. Trying to meet in person.

sections.heading: What Parents Should Watch For
sections.body: Sudden secrecy about online activity. New apps or accounts they won't explain. Receiving gifts from strangers. Becoming withdrawn or defensive about online friends. Older 'friends' who don't know their peers.

sections.heading: How to Respond
sections.body: Stay calm. Keep communication open. Document everything. Do not confront the suspected person yourself. Report to NCMEC at report.cybertrip.org and local law enforcement.

quiz.question: Which of these is a common grooming warning sign?
options: Asking the child to keep secrets
quiz.answer: Asking the child to keep secrets

quiz.question: What should you do if you suspect grooming?
options: Wait to see if it stops
quiz.answer: Document and report to NCMEC

quiz.question: Grooming typically happens:
quiz.answer: Gradually over time

lessons.title: Privacy Settings & Digital Boundaries
lessons.summary: How to lock down accounts, set boundaries, and protect personal information.

sections.heading: Why Privacy Settings Matter
sections.body: Predators use public information to learn about children. A public profile can reveal a child's location, school, friends, routines, and personal interests.

sections.heading: Essential Settings to Check
sections.body: Set profiles to private. Disable location sharing. Turn off geotagging on photos. Limit who can message or friend your child. Review tagged photos before they go live.

sections.heading: Platform-Specific Tips
sections.body: On Instagram: Private account + close friends for stories. On TikTok: Private account + disable comments on videos. On Discord: Restrict direct messages to friends only. On Snapchat: Disable quick add and location sharing.

sections.heading: Digital Boundaries at Home
sections.body: Use device screen time limits. Keep devices in common areas. Set rules about sharing passwords. Discuss what information is never okay to share: full name, address, school name, phone number.

quiz.question: What is the most basic privacy setting every child should have?
quiz.answer: Private profile

quiz.question: Which information should never be shared publicly?
quiz.answer: School name and address

quiz.question: Geotagging on photos:
options: Is always safe to leave on
Should be reviewed and often disabled for children
quiz.answer: Should be reviewed and often disabled for children

lessons.title: Safe Online Communication
lessons.summary: Guidelines for messaging, video chats, and online friendships.

sections.heading: Stranger Danger Online
sections.body: People online are not always who they say they are. A profile saying '15-year-old boy' could be anyone. Teach children to never assume a stranger's identity is real.

sections.heading: Rules for Messaging
sections.body: Never share personal information. Never send private photos. Never agree to video chat with someone you haven't met in person. Tell a trusted adult if anyone makes you uncomfortable.

sections.heading: Recognizing Fake Profiles
sections.body: Few friends or followers. Recently created account. Stock photos instead of real photos. Inconsistent details. Refuses to video call. Always has an excuse not to meet in person.

sections.heading: What to Do When Something Feels Wrong
sections.body: Stop communicating. Save screenshots. Block the person. Tell a trusted adult. Report the profile to the platform and to NCMEC.

quiz.question: What should a child do if an online stranger makes them uncomfortable?
options: Stop, screenshot, block, tell an adult
quiz.answer: Stop, screenshot, block, tell an adult

quiz.question: Fake profiles often have:
quiz.answer: Recent account + stock photos

quiz.question: It is safe to video chat with an online friend you have:
options: Known for 2 years but never met
Only met in a monitored setting with parent present
quiz.answer: Only met in a monitored setting with parent present

lessons.title: How to Report Suspicious Activity
lessons.summary: Step-by-step guide to reporting online exploitation to the proper authorities.

sections.heading: Why Reporting Matters
sections.body: Every report helps connect cases, identify patterns, and build evidence against offenders. You may be the only one who noticed.

sections.heading: What to Report
sections.body: Unsolicited sexual messages. Requests for private photos. Anyone asking a child to keep secrets. Profiles that seem to be impersonating a child. Known CSAM (child sexual abuse material).

sections.heading: How to Document Evidence
sections.body: Take screenshots with timestamps. Save URLs and usernames. Record dates and times. Do NOT share or distribute the material. Send your documentation directly to law enforcement.

sections.heading: Reporting Channels
sections.body: NCMEC CyberTipline: report.cybertrip.org. FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3): ic3.gov. Local police non-emergency line. Platform reporting tools (each platform has a report feature).

quiz.question: What is the primary reporting channel for online child exploitation?
quiz.answer: NCMEC CyberTipline

quiz.question: When documenting evidence, you should:
options: Share it online to warn others
Take screenshots with timestamps and URLs
quiz.answer: Take screenshots with timestamps and URLs

quiz.question: If you find CSAM online, you should:
options: Download and save it for proof
Report to NCMEC — do not share or distribute
Post about it on social media
Contact the person who posted it
quiz.answer: Report to NCMEC — do not share or distribute

lessons.title: Password Security & Account Safety
lessons.summary: How to create strong passwords and keep accounts secure from compromise.

sections.heading: Why Account Security Matters
sections.body: Compromised accounts can be used to impersonate your child, access private conversations, and gather personal information. Strong passwords are the first line of defense.

sections.heading: Creating Strong Passwords
sections.body: Use at least 12 characters. Mix upper and lowercase letters, numbers, and symbols. Never use personal info (birthdays, names). Use a passphrase: 'CorrectHorseBatteryStaple' style. Use a password manager.

sections.heading: Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)
sections.body: Enable 2FA on every account that supports it. Use an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy) rather than SMS when possible. This prevents someone from logging in even if they have the password.

sections.heading: Recognizing Phishing
sections.body: Don't click links in unsolicited messages. Check the sender's email address carefully. Look for misspellings and odd URLs. When in doubt, go directly to the website instead of clicking the link.

quiz.question: A strong password should be:
options: At least 12 characters with mixed symbols
quiz.answer: At least 12 characters with mixed symbols

quiz.question: Two-factor authentication:
options: Should be enabled on every account that supports it
quiz.answer: Should be enabled on every account that supports it

quiz.question: If you get a suspicious link from a friend's account:
options: Click it — it's from a friend
Don't click — their account may be compromised
quiz.answer: Don't click — their account may be compromised

lessons.title: Social Media Platform Safety
lessons.summary: Deep dive into safety features across major social platforms.

sections.heading: Instagram
sections.body: Private accounts, close friends lists, comment filters, blocked accounts, and restricted mode. Review tagged photos manually. Disable message requests from strangers.

sections.heading: TikTok
sections.body: Private account, disable duet/stitch for minors, restricted mode, screen time limits, comment filtering. Family Pairing lets parents link their account to their teen's.

sections.heading: Snapchat
sections.body: Disable quick add. Enable ghost mode on Snap Map. Restrict story visibility to friends only. Disable 'see me in quick add'. Review friend list regularly.

sections.heading: Discord
sections.body: Set direct messages to friends only. Disable server DMs. Enable explicit content filter. Review joined servers regularly. Set up family-friendly servers.

quiz.question: Which TikTok feature lets parents link their account to their teen's?
quiz.answer: Family Pairing

quiz.question: On Snapchat, what should you do to protect location privacy?
options: Enable ghost mode on Snap Map
quiz.answer: Enable ghost mode on Snap Map

quiz.question: On Discord, who should be able to DM your child?
quiz.answer: Only friends

lessons.title: Cyberbullying Recognition & Response
lessons.summary: Identify cyberbullying and take effective action to protect your child.

sections.heading: What is Cyberbullying?
sections.body: Cyberbullying is bullying that happens through digital devices. It includes mean texts, rumors spread online, embarrassing photos or videos, fake profiles, and exclusion from group chats.

sections.heading: Warning Signs
sections.body: Sudden withdrawal from devices. Visible distress after using phone/computer. Hiding screens when parents approach. Declining grades. Changes in sleep or eating. Avoiding school or social events.

sections.heading: How to Respond
sections.body: Don't respond to the bully. Save all evidence (screenshots, dates, URLs). Block the bully. Report to the platform. Inform the school if involved. Support your child emotionally.

sections.heading: Prevention Strategies
sections.body: Teach your child to never share passwords. Encourage them to come to you if anything happens. Role-play scenarios. Set clear expectations about kind online behavior.

quiz.question: Which is NOT a sign of cyberbullying?
quiz.answer: More excited about using devices

quiz.question: The first step when your child experiences cyberbullying is:
options: Save evidence and don't respond to the bully
Post about it on social media
quiz.answer: Save evidence and don't respond to the bully

quiz.question: Cyberbullying includes:
options: Mean texts, rumors, embarrassing photos, and exclusion
quiz.answer: Mean texts, rumors, embarrassing photos, and exclusion

lessons.title: Digital Footprint Management
lessons.summary: Understand and control your child's long-term online presence.

sections.heading: What is a Digital Footprint?
sections.body: Everything posted online creates a permanent record. Comments, photos, likes, shares, and even search history contribute to a digital footprint that colleges, employers, and others can find.

sections.heading: Why It Matters for Children
sections.body: Content posted today can resurface years later. Embarrassing photos, inappropriate comments, or oversharing can affect college admissions, job offers, and personal relationships.

sections.heading: How to Manage It
sections.body: Google your child's name regularly. Set up Google Alerts. Review and remove old content. Set strict privacy on all accounts. Teach that the internet never forgets.

sections.heading: The Pause Principle
sections.body: Before posting anything, pause and ask: Would I want a grandparent, teacher, or future employer to see this? If not, don't post it. A moment of pause prevents years of regret.

quiz.question: What is a digital footprint?
quiz.body: The permanent record of everything posted online
options: The permanent record of everything posted online
quiz.answer: The permanent record of everything posted online

quiz.question: Who might look at a digital footprint?
options: Colleges, employers, and anyone with internet access
quiz.answer: Colleges, employers, and anyone with internet access

quiz.question: The 'Pause Principle' means:
options: Taking a break from social media
Pausing before posting to consider future consequences
quiz.answer: Pausing before posting to consider future consequences

lessons.title: Mobile App Safety
lessons.summary: Evaluate apps for safety and set up parental controls on mobile devices.

sections.heading: App Risks
sections.body: Not all apps are what they seem. Some collect excessive data. Some have hidden adult content. Some facilitate anonymous communication with strangers. Some have weak privacy controls.

sections.heading: How to Evaluate an App
sections.body: Read privacy policies. Check age ratings and reviews. Test it yourself before allowing your child. Look at what data it collects and who it shares with. Check if it has reporting features.

sections.heading: Parental Controls by Platform
sections.body: iOS: Screen Time with content restrictions, app limits, communication limits. Android: Family Link for app approval, screen time, content filtering. Third-party: Qustodio, Bark, Norton Family.

sections.heading: Dangerous App Categories
sections.body: Anonymous messaging apps. Apps that hide content (vault apps). Apps with no age verification. Apps with unmoderated chat rooms. Apps that encourage secret communication.

quiz.question: Before letting your child use a new app, you should:
options: Test it yourself and check privacy settings
Only check the number of downloads
quiz.answer: Test it yourself and check privacy settings

quiz.question: Vault apps are dangerous because:
options: They can hide content and secret communications
quiz.answer: They can hide content and secret communications

quiz.question: Which app category is highest risk?
quiz.answer: Anonymous messaging apps

lessons.title: When & How to Talk to Children
lessons.summary: Age-appropriate conversations about online safety that build trust.

sections.heading: Start Early (Ages 4-7)
sections.body: Use simple concepts. 'The internet is like a big city — there are fun places and some places we don't go alone.' Teach that they can always come to you. Introduce the idea that not everyone online is who they say they are.

sections.heading: Pre-Teen (Ages 8-12)
sections.body: Have direct conversations about social media, privacy, and strangers online. Set clear rules and explain why they exist. Discuss what information is private. Role-play scenarios.

sections.heading: Teen (Ages 13+)
sections.body: Shift to guiding rather than controlling. Discuss digital reputation, consent, and critical thinking. Talk about healthy relationships online. Be honest about risks without being alarmist. Respect their privacy while maintaining oversight.

sections.heading: Keeping Communication Open
sections.body: Don't punish them for coming to you with problems. Have regular, casual check-ins. Use 'teachable moments' from news or media. Let them teach you about new apps. Make it safe for them to be honest.

quiz.question: For children ages 4-7, conversations about online safety should be:
quiz.answer: Simple with analogies they understand

quiz.question: The most important factor for teens is:
quiz.answer: Open communication and guidance

quiz.question: If your child comes to you with an online problem, you should:
options: Punish them for using the app
Thank them and help without punishing
Tell them to handle it themselves
quiz.answer: Thank them and help without punishing
